q: What are the three components of the cardiovascular system?
a: 1. Heart (pump) 2. Blood (fluid) 3. Blood vessels: Arteries, Veins, Capillaries

q: What materials does the cardiovascular system transport FROM the external environment?
a: Nutrients, water, and gases (e.g., oxygen from lungs, nutrients from intestinal tract — delivered to all cells)

q: What materials does the cardiovascular system transport BETWEEN cells?
a: Hormones (endocrine cells to target cells), immune cells/antibodies/clotting proteins (continuously present in blood), and stored nutrients (from liver and adipose tissue to all cells)

q: What waste materials does the cardiovascular system transport OUT of the body?
a: Metabolic wastes (all cells to kidneys), CO2 (all cells to lungs), Heat (all cells to skin)

q: What is a portal system in the cardiovascular context?
a: A portal system joins two capillary beds in series — blood passes through one capillary bed, then a portal vessel, then a second capillary bed before returning to the heart (e.g., hepatic portal system)

q: What is the function of the cardiac septum?
a: The septum divides the heart into two halves (left and right), keeping oxygenated and deoxygenated blood separate

q: What is the difference between an atrium and a ventricle in the heart?
a: Atria are thin-walled upper chambers that receive blood returning to the heart. Ventricles are thick-walled lower chambers that pump blood out of the heart.

q: What is the myocardium and what encases it?
a: The myocardium is the cardiac muscle that makes up most of the heart wall. It is encased in the pericardium (a fibrous sac surrounding the heart).

q: What is the difference between pulmonary circulation and systemic circulation?
a: Pulmonary circulation: right heart pumps blood to the lungs via pulmonary arteries; oxygenated blood returns via pulmonary veins. Systemic circulation: left heart pumps oxygenated blood to the body via the aorta; deoxygenated blood returns via the venae cavae.

q: What is the pathway of DEOXYGENATED blood through the heart?
a: Vena cava → Right atrium → Right ventricle → Pulmonary trunk → Lungs

q: What is the pathway of OXYGENATED blood through the heart?
a: Pulmonary veins → Left atrium → Left ventricle → Aorta → Body

q: In what direction does blood flow, and why?
a: Blood flows from regions of HIGHER pressure to regions of LOWER pressure. The contraction of the heart creates the pressure gradient that drives this flow.

q: What is the mean blood pressure in the aorta, and what is it in the venae cavae?
a: Aorta: approximately 93 mm Hg (highest). Venae cavae: a few mm Hg (lowest). Blood flows down this pressure gradient from aorta through arteries, arterioles, capillaries, venules, veins, to venae cavae.

q: What is a pressure gradient (deltaP) and what does it determine?
a: A pressure gradient (deltaP) is the difference in pressure between two regions (P1 - P2). Flow is directly proportional to deltaP: the higher the pressure gradient, the greater the fluid flow (Flow proportional to deltaP).

q: Does blood flow depend on absolute pressure or on the pressure GRADIENT?
a: Blood flow depends on the PRESSURE GRADIENT (deltaP), NOT on absolute pressure. Two tubes with different absolute pressures but equal deltaP will have equal flow.

q: What is hydrostatic pressure?
a: Hydrostatic pressure is the pressure exerted by a fluid even if it is NOT in motion. It is exerted in all directions.

q: What is driving pressure?
a: Driving pressure is the pressure created by the contraction of the heart that pushes blood out into vessels. Contraction creates pressure WITHOUT changing the volume of blood.

q: State Poiseuille's Law and its formula for resistance.
a: Poiseuille's Law: R = 8Ln / (pi x r^4), or R proportional to Ln/r^4. Resistance increases with tube LENGTH (L) and fluid VISCOSITY (n); resistance decreases dramatically as tube RADIUS (r) increases (inversely proportional to the 4th power of radius).

q: According to Poiseuille's Law, how does radius affect resistance? Give a numerical example.
a: Resistance is inversely proportional to radius to the FOURTH power (R proportional to 1/r^4). Example: Tube A (radius=1) has Flow proportional to 1; Tube B (radius=2) has Flow proportional to 16. Doubling the radius increases flow 16-fold.

q: What is the combined flow relationship incorporating pressure gradient AND resistance?
a: Flow proportional to deltaP/R. Flow increases as pressure gradient increases (directly proportional) AND as resistance decreases (inversely proportional).

q: What is the formula for velocity of flow, and how does vessel cross-sectional area affect it?
a: Velocity (v) = Flow rate (Q) / Cross-sectional area (A). A NARROWER vessel has FASTER velocity for the same flow rate (e.g., area=1 cm^2 gives v=12 cm/min; area=12 cm^2 gives v=1 cm/min at same Q=12 cm^3/min).

q: What is Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP) and what is its formula?
a: Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP) is the primary driving force for blood flow — the pressure reserved in the arteries during heart relaxation. Formula: MAP = Cardiac Output (CO) x Peripheral Resistance (PR).

q: What are the 6 structural differences between cardiac muscle cells and skeletal muscle cells?
a: 1. Smaller and have a SINGLE nucleus per fiber (skeletal is multinucleated). 2. Branch and join neighboring cells through intercalated disks. 3. Contain GAP JUNCTIONS. 4. T-tubules are LARGER and branch. 5. Sarcoplasmic reticulum is SMALLER. 6. Mitochondria occupy ONE-THIRD of cell volume (more than skeletal).

q: What is the functional significance of the SPIRAL arrangement of ventricular muscle?
a: The spiral arrangement of ventricular muscle allows ventricular contraction to SQUEEZE blood UPWARD from the apex of the heart toward the outflow tracts.

q: What two structures are found in intercalated disks and what are their functions?
a: 1. DESMOSOMES: transfer mechanical force from cell to cell. 2. GAP JUNCTIONS: allow electrical signals to pass rapidly from cell to cell (electrical syncytium).

q: What are AUTORHYTHMIC cells (pacemaker cells)? List 3 key properties.
a: Autorhythmic cells are specialized cardiac cells that can initiate action potentials WITHOUT a motor neuron. Properties: 1. No true resting potential (unstable pacemaker potential). 2. Fewer and smaller contractile fibers. 3. No organized sarcomeres.

q: What are CONTRACTILE cells (myocardial muscle cells)? How do they differ from autorhythmic cells?
a: Contractile cells have striated fibers organized into SARCOMERES. They are responsible for force generation. Unlike autorhythmic cells, they have a stable resting membrane potential (-90 mV) and do not spontaneously depolarize.

q: Where is the SA node located, what is its intrinsic firing rate, and what is its function?
a: Location: Upper right atrium. Intrinsic rate: 70 beats per minute (bpm). Function: Primary pacemaker of the heart — initiates each heartbeat under normal conditions.

q: Where is the AV node located, what is its intrinsic rate, and what is its key functional role?
a: Location: Junction between atria and ventricles. Intrinsic rate: 50 bpm. Key role: Creates a 0.1-second DELAY in conduction — allowing the atria to finish contracting before ventricular contraction begins. Acts as a secondary pacemaker if the SA node fails.

q: What is the AV bundle (Bundle of His) and where does it conduct?
a: The AV bundle (Bundle of His) connects the AV node to the ventricles. It conducts impulses from the AV node through the interventricular septum, then divides into the LEFT and RIGHT bundle branches.

q: What are the bundle branches and what do they lead to?
a: The bundle branches are the left and right extensions of the AV bundle that run down both sides of the interventricular septum. They lead to the PURKINJE FIBERS (subendocardial conducting network) which stimulate ventricular contractile cells.

q: What are Purkinje fibers, what is their intrinsic rate, and what do they stimulate?
a: Purkinje fibers are the terminal branches of the ventricular conducting system (subendocardial conducting network). Intrinsic rate: 25-40 bpm. They stimulate the contractile cells of BOTH ventricles, causing contraction from the apex upward.

q: What is the internodal pathway?
a: The internodal pathway is the conduction route from the SA node to the AV node through atrial tissue. It routes the direction of electrical signals so the heart contracts from the APEX to the BASE.

q: List the 5 sequential steps of electrical conduction through the heart.
a: Step 1: SA node depolarizes. Step 2: Electrical activity travels RAPIDLY to the AV node via internodal pathways. Step 3: Depolarization spreads more slowly across atria; conduction SLOWS through the AV node (0.1 sec delay). Step 4: Depolarization moves RAPIDLY through ventricular conducting system to the APEX. Step 5: Depolarization wave spreads UPWARD from the apex.

q: WHY does conduction slow at the AV node?
a: The AV node delay (0.1 sec) ensures that the ATRIA FINISH CONTRACTING before ventricular contraction begins, allowing maximum ventricular filling before the ventricles pump.

q: Describe the EC coupling CONTRACTION phase in cardiac muscle (overview).
a: 1. AP travels along sarcolemma and down T-tubules. 2. Voltage-gated L-type Ca2+ channels (DHP receptors) open — small Ca2+ influx. 3. Small Ca2+ influx triggers Ca2+ release from SR via ryanodine receptors (RyR) — this is CALCIUM-INDUCED CALCIUM RELEASE. 4. Elevated cytosolic Ca2+ binds troponin → contraction.

q: What are voltage-gated L-type Ca2+ channels (DHP receptors) in cardiac muscle?
a: L-type Ca2+ channels (also called DHP receptors) are voltage-gated channels in the T-tubule membrane. They open during depolarization, allowing a SMALL initial influx of Ca2+ into the cytosol. This small Ca2+ spark then triggers the much larger Ca2+ release from the SR via RyR.

q: What are ryanodine receptors (RyR) and what is their role in cardiac EC coupling?
a: Ryanodine receptors (RyR) are Ca2+-release channels located on the sarcoplasmic reticulum (SR) membrane. They are triggered by the small Ca2+ influx from L-type channels (calcium-induced calcium release), causing a MUCH LARGER release of stored Ca2+ from the SR into the cytosol.

q: What is calcium-induced calcium release (CICR)?
a: Calcium-induced calcium release (CICR) is the mechanism in cardiac EC coupling where a SMALL Ca2+ influx through L-type Ca2+ channels triggers a MUCH LARGER Ca2+ release from the SR via ryanodine receptors. Local releases create Ca2+ sparks that sum to produce a Ca2+ signal strong enough for contraction.

q: List all 10 steps of cardiac EC coupling (from SUP1 detailed figure).
a: 1. AP enters from adjacent cell. 2. Voltage-gated Ca2+ channels open; Ca2+ enters cell. 3. Ca2+ induces Ca2+ release through RyR. 4. Local release causes Ca2+ spark. 5. Summed Ca2+ sparks create a Ca2+ signal. 6. Ca2+ binds troponin to initiate contraction. 7. Relaxation occurs when Ca2+ unbinds from troponin. 8. Ca2+ pumped back into SR (SERCA). 9. Ca2+ exchanged for Na+ by NCX antiporter. 10. Na+ gradient maintained by Na+-K+-ATPase.

q: How is Ca2+ removed from the cytosol during cardiac RELAXATION (2 mechanisms)?
a: 1. SERCA pump (Ca2+-ATPase): pumps Ca2+ BACK INTO the SR for storage. 2. Na+/Ca2+ exchanger (NCX) and Ca2+-ATPase: pump Ca2+ OUT OF the cell (NCX brings 3 Na+ in and moves 1 Ca2+ out; Na+ gradient maintained by Na+/K+-ATPase).

q: What is Phase 4 of the CONTRACTILE cardiac cell action potential?
a: Phase 4 is the resting membrane potential: STABLE at -90 mV. The membrane is at equilibrium with K+ channels dominating. This is the starting point before the next AP arrives via gap junctions.

q: What is Phase 0 of the contractile cardiac cell action potential?
a: Phase 0 is RAPID DEPOLARIZATION: fast voltage-gated Na+ channels OPEN, causing a rapid upswing from -90 mV toward +20 mV. P_Na increases sharply.

q: What is Phase 1 of the contractile cardiac cell action potential?
a: Phase 1 is INITIAL REPOLARIZATION: fast Na+ channels CLOSE, causing a brief, slight dip in membrane potential. P_Na decreases. This is very brief before the plateau begins.

q: What is Phase 2 of the contractile cardiac cell action potential?
a: Phase 2 is the PLATEAU: Ca2+ channels OPEN (P_Ca increases) and fast K+ channels CLOSE (P_K decreases). The Ca2+ influx balances K+ efflux, holding the membrane potential relatively stable for ~200 msec. This prolonged depolarization drives EC coupling.

q: What is Phase 3 of the contractile cardiac cell action potential?
a: Phase 3 is RAPID REPOLARIZATION: Ca2+ channels CLOSE (P_Ca decreases) and slow K+ channels OPEN (P_K increases). K+ efflux drives the membrane potential back toward -90 mV.

q: Why can cardiac muscle NOT undergo tetanus?
a: Cardiac muscle has a LONG REFRACTORY PERIOD that lasts almost as long as the entire muscle twitch (~200-250 msec). A new stimulus cannot cause a second contraction until the first has nearly completely relaxed. This prevents summation and tetanus, ensuring the heart can always relax to fill with blood.

q: How does the refractory period of cardiac muscle differ from that of skeletal muscle?
a: Cardiac muscle: refractory period is VERY LONG (~200-250 msec), lasting almost as long as the muscle twitch; tetanus is NOT possible. Skeletal muscle: refractory period is VERY SHORT compared to twitch duration; summation and tetanus ARE possible with repeated stimuli.

q: What is a pacemaker potential and what is its starting voltage?
a: A pacemaker potential is the UNSTABLE, spontaneously depolarizing membrane potential of autorhythmic cells. Unlike contractile cells, there is NO TRUE RESTING POTENTIAL. The pacemaker potential starts at approximately -60 mV and slowly drifts upward toward threshold.

q: What are 'funny' Na+ channels (If channels) and what role do they play in the pacemaker potential?
a: Funny (If) channels are unique Na+ channels that OPEN at NEGATIVE (hyperpolarized) membrane potentials in autorhythmic cells. They open at approximately -60 mV and allow a slow Na+ influx, causing the gradual depolarization of the pacemaker potential from -60 mV toward threshold.

q: What is the threshold of autorhythmic cells and what event triggers the action potential?
a: Threshold of autorhythmic cells: approximately -40 mV. Near threshold, SOME Ca2+ channels begin to open, reinforcing depolarization while If channels close. When -40 mV is reached, MANY Ca2+ channels open rapidly, triggering the action potential.

q: What is Phase 0 of the AUTORHYTHMIC cell action potential, and why is it different from contractile cells?
a: Phase 0 (depolarization): LOTS of Ca2+ channels open, causing a slower upstroke. Na+ channels are ABSENT in autorhythmic cells, so depolarization is carried entirely by Ca2+ (slower and smaller amplitude than contractile cell Phase 0).

q: What is Phase 3 of the AUTORHYTHMIC cell action potential?
a: Phase 3 (repolarization): K+ channels OPEN, K+ efflux repolarizes the cell back toward -60 mV. Ca2+ channels CLOSE as the potential returns to -60 mV. At -60 mV, If channels open again and the next pacemaker potential cycle begins.

q: Table 14.3: Compare RESTING MEMBRANE POTENTIAL across skeletal, contractile myocardium, and autorhythmic myocardium.
a: Skeletal muscle: Stable at -70 to -80 mV. Contractile myocardium: Stable at -90 mV. Autorhythmic myocardium: UNSTABLE pacemaker potential, typically starts at -60 mV.

q: Table 14.3: Compare the RISING PHASE of the action potential in skeletal, contractile, and autorhythmic myocardium.
a: Skeletal muscle: Na+ entry. Contractile myocardium: Na+ entry. Autorhythmic myocardium: Ca2+ entry (no fast Na+ channels present).

q: Table 14.3: Compare REPOLARIZATION in contractile myocardium vs. skeletal and autorhythmic myocardium.
a: Contractile myocardium: Extended PLATEAU phase due to Ca2+ entry; rapid phase then caused by K+ efflux (duration 200+ msec). Skeletal muscle: Rapid repolarization by K+ efflux (1-2 msec). Autorhythmic myocardium: Rapid repolarization by K+ efflux (variable, generally 150+ msec).

q: Table 14.3: Compare the REFRACTORY PERIOD of contractile myocardium vs. skeletal muscle.
a: Contractile myocardium: LONG refractory period (because resetting of Na+ channel gates is delayed until end of action potential) — prevents tetanus. Skeletal muscle: GENERALLY BRIEF refractory period relative to twitch — allows summation and tetanus.

q: How do action potentials SPREAD from autorhythmic cells to contractile cells?
a: Autorhythmic cells SPONTANEOUSLY fire action potentials. Depolarizations spread RAPIDLY to adjacent contractile cells through GAP JUNCTIONS located at INTERCALATED DISKS. Electrical current flows directly between cells, making cardiac muscle function as an electrical syncytium.

q: What are intercalated disks and what do they contain?
a: Intercalated disks are specialized cell-to-cell junctions between cardiac muscle cells. They contain: 1. GAP JUNCTIONS — for rapid electrical signal passage. 2. DESMOSOMES — for mechanical force transfer between cells.

q: What are the two main coronary arteries and their branches (anterior surface)?
a: 1. LEFT CORONARY ARTERY (LCA): divides into (a) Circumflex branch and (b) Anterior interventricular branch / Left Anterior Descending (LAD). 2. RIGHT CORONARY ARTERY (RCA): gives rise to posterior interventricular branch (posterior surface). Venous drainage is via the coronary sinus.

q: Define DIASTOLE and SYSTOLE.
a: DIASTOLE: Phase of the cardiac cycle in which cardiac muscle RELAXES; chambers fill with blood. SYSTOLE: Phase of the cardiac cycle in which cardiac muscle CONTRACTS; blood is pumped out.

q: What are the FIVE phases of the cardiac cycle (names only)?
a: 1. Late diastole. 2. Atrial systole. 3. Isovolumic ventricular contraction. 4. Ventricular ejection. 5. Isovolumic ventricular relaxation.

q: Phase 1 — Late Diastole: What happens during this phase?
a: Both atria AND ventricles are RELAXED. The atria fill with returning blood. AV valves are OPEN, so blood passively flows into the ventricles. Approximately 80% of ventricular filling occurs PASSIVELY during this phase.

q: Phase 2 — Atrial Systole: What happens and what volume is established?
a: The ATRIA CONTRACT, pushing the FINAL 20% of blood into the ventricles. This establishes the END-DIASTOLIC VOLUME (EDV) — the maximum volume of blood in the ventricle, measured at the end of ventricular relaxation.

q: What is End-Diastolic Volume (EDV) and what is its approximate value?
a: EDV is the volume of blood in the ventricle at the END of ventricular RELAXATION (= maximum volume). Established after atrial systole completes filling. Approximate value: ~135 mL (per Wiggers diagram).

q: Phase 3 — Isovolumic Ventricular Contraction: What happens and what sound occurs?
a: Ventricular contraction begins; AV valves CLOSE (producing S1 'Lub'). Ventricular pressure rises, but no valves are open so NO blood enters or exits (isovolumic = constant volume). Concurrent: atria relax (atrial diastole) and begin refilling.

q: Why is Phase 3 called 'isovolumic'?
a: 'Isovolumic' means the volume DOES NOT CHANGE. During Phase 3, all four heart valves are CLOSED. Ventricular pressure is rising due to muscle contraction, but it has not yet exceeded arterial pressure, so semilunar valves cannot open. Volume stays constant at EDV.

q: Phase 4 — Ventricular Ejection: What happens and what volume is established?
a: Ventricular pressure rises until it EXCEEDS arterial pressure; semilunar valves OPEN. Blood is ejected into the arteries. This establishes the END-SYSTOLIC VOLUME (ESV) — the minimum volume of blood remaining in the ventricle after contraction.

q: What is End-Systolic Volume (ESV) and what is its approximate value?
a: ESV is the volume of blood remaining in the ventricle at the END of ventricular CONTRACTION (= minimum volume; not all blood is ejected). Approximate value: ~65 mL (per Wiggers diagram).

q: Phase 5 — Isovolumic Ventricular Relaxation: What happens and what sound occurs?
a: Ventricles relax; arterial blood flows back toward the heart, filling the cusps of semilunar valves and SNAPPING THEM SHUT (producing S2 'Dup'). Volume stays constant (all valves closed). AV valves open WHEN ventricular pressure drops below atrial pressure, beginning the next cycle.

q: What is S1 ('Lub') — when does it occur and what causes it?
a: S1 is the FIRST heart sound. Cause: CLOSURE of the AV valves (tricuspid + mitral). Timing: Occurs at the BEGINNING of ventricular SYSTOLE (Phase 3 onset). Represents the transition from ventricular filling to ventricular contraction.

q: What is S2 ('Dup') — when does it occur and what causes it?
a: S2 is the SECOND heart sound. Cause: CLOSURE of the semilunar valves (aortic + pulmonary). Timing: Occurs at the END of ventricular SYSTOLE / beginning of ventricular DIASTOLE (Phase 5 onset). Represents the transition from ejection to ventricular relaxation.

q: What is the sequence of heart sounds during a NORMAL cardiac cycle?
a: S1 ('Lub') — S2 ('Dup') — [silence] — S1 ('Lub')... Normal blood flow is LAMINAR (smooth, non-turbulent) and therefore SILENT between the two valve sounds.

q: What is an electrocardiogram (ECG)?
a: An ECG records the SUMMED ELECTRICAL ACTIVITY generated by ALL cells of the heart, recorded from electrodes on the body surface. It is NOT an action potential of a single cell. Mechanical events (contractions) LAG BEHIND the electrical events shown on the ECG.

q: What does the P WAVE of the ECG represent?
a: The P wave represents ATRIAL DEPOLARIZATION. The SA node fires, depolarization spreads across both atria. Atrial CONTRACTION begins at the END of the P wave.

q: What does the QRS COMPLEX of the ECG represent?
a: The QRS complex represents VENTRICULAR DEPOLARIZATION. Ventricular contraction begins at the end of the Q wave and continues through the T wave. NOTE: Atrial REPOLARIZATION is also occurring during QRS but is buried within/masked by the much larger QRS signal.

q: What does the T WAVE of the ECG represent?
a: The T wave represents VENTRICULAR REPOLARIZATION. Ventricular muscle is recovering from depolarization. The ventricles begin to RELAX (ventricular diastole) during and after the T wave.

q: What does the P-R SEGMENT of the ECG represent?
a: The P-R segment represents the AV NODAL DELAY — the time for conduction through the AV node and AV bundle. The ECG returns to baseline (isoelectric) during this period. It represents slowed conduction that ensures atria finish contracting before ventricular systole.

q: What does the T-P SEGMENT of the ECG represent?
a: The T-P segment represents VENTRICULAR AND ATRIAL RELAXATION. It is the isoelectric (baseline) period between the end of the T wave and the beginning of the next P wave.

q: What does the S-T SEGMENT of the ECG represent?
a: The S-T segment is the flat isoelectric line between the S wave and the T wave. It corresponds to the period when the VENTRICLES ARE FULLY DEPOLARIZED (all ventricular cells in plateau phase) and actively contracting.

q: What is the PR INTERVAL and the QT INTERVAL?
a: PR interval: From the START of the P wave to the START of the QRS complex (includes P wave + P-R segment). QT interval: From the START of the QRS complex to the END of the T wave (spans entire ventricular electrical activity).

q: What is the standard ECG paper scale?
a: Horizontal: 5 mm = one small box; 25 mm = 1 second. Vertical: +1 millivolt at top of y-axis. This allows calculation of heart rate and measurement of interval durations.

q: Define TACHYCARDIA, BRADYCARDIA, and normal SINUS RHYTHM (with bpm values).
a: Sinus rhythm (normal): 60-100 bpm. Tachycardia: heart rate FASTER than normal (greater than 100 bpm). Bradycardia: heart rate SLOWER than normal (less than 60 bpm). Heart rate is measured as the time between two consecutive P waves or R waves.

q: What is an ECG LEAD and what are the three configurations of Einthoven's Triangle (Leads I, II, III)?
a: A lead is a specific VIEWPOINT of cardiac electrical activity, recording the voltage difference between two electrodes. Lead I: Right arm (-) to Left arm (+). Lead II: Right arm (-) to Left leg (+). Lead III: Left arm (-) to Left leg (+). Together these form Einthoven's Triangle.

q: What does a 12-lead ECG provide vs. a 3-lead ECG?
a: A 12-lead ECG uses 12 different electrode viewpoints to provide a THREE-DIMENSIONAL view of cardiac electrical activity, allowing more precise localization of arrhythmias and infarcts. The classic system uses 3 leads (Einthoven's Triangle); the current clinical standard is the 12-lead ECG.

q: What are the three ECG deflection rules based on current flow direction relative to electrodes?
a: Rule 1: UPWARD deflection = current vector moving TOWARD the positive electrode. Rule 2: DOWNWARD deflection = current vector moving toward the NEGATIVE electrode. Rule 3: NO deflection (baseline) = current vector is PERPENDICULAR to the electrode axis.

q: What are the 4 questions to ask when interpreting an ECG tracing?
a: 1. What is the RATE? Is it within the normal range of 60-100 bpm? 2. Is the RHYTHM regular or irregular? 3. Are all normal WAVES present in recognizable form? 4. Is there ONE QRS complex for each P wave? If yes, is the P-R segment CONSTANT in length?

q: What is THIRD-DEGREE BLOCK and what does its ECG look like?
a: Third-degree block (complete heart block): The AV node FAILS to conduct any impulses from atria to ventricles. The atria and ventricles beat INDEPENDENTLY at different rates. ECG shows P waves (atrial rate) and R waves (ventricular rate) that are DISSOCIATED — more P waves than QRS complexes.

q: What is ATRIAL FIBRILLATION and what does its ECG look like?
a: Atrial fibrillation: CHAOTIC, disorganized atrial electrical activity. ECG shows: irregular rapid small oscillations (no distinct P waves), irregular R-R intervals (irregular ventricular response). The atria quiver rather than contract in a coordinated fashion.

q: What is VENTRICULAR FIBRILLATION and what does its ECG look like?
a: Ventricular fibrillation: CHAOTIC, disorganized ventricular electrical activity — a life-threatening emergency. ECG shows: completely chaotic irregular waveforms with NO identifiable P, QRS, or T waves. Results in cardiac output of zero.

q: What is the dicrotic notch on the aortic pressure tracing?
a: The dicrotic notch is a brief DOWNWARD NOTCH visible in the aortic pressure tracing. It corresponds to the CLOSURE of the AORTIC SEMILUNAR VALVE (producing S2). It represents the momentary backflow of blood into the aortic valve cusps before they snap shut.

q: Describe the 4 panels of the Wiggers Diagram (what each panel shows).
a: Panel 1 (top): ECG (P wave, QRS, T wave). Panel 2: Pressure (mm Hg) — shows aortic pressure, left ventricular pressure, and left atrial pressure with labeled events (S1, S2, dicrotic notch). Panel 3: Heart sounds (S1 at start of systole, S2 at end of systole). Panel 4 (bottom): Left ventricular volume (mL) — rises to EDV (~135 mL), drops to ESV (~65 mL).

q: On the Pressure-Volume loop, identify what happens at each labeled point: A, A', B, C, and D.
a: A (START): ~65 mL, low pressure (ESV; AV valve opens). A to A': Ventricular FILLING (diastole); volume rises to ~135 mL. B (EDV, ~135 mL): AV valve closes; isovolumic contraction begins; pressure rises. C: Semilunar valves OPEN; ejection begins. D (ESV, ~65 mL): Semilunar valves CLOSE; isovolumic relaxation begins. D back to A: Pressure falls at constant volume.

q: What is the formula for STROKE VOLUME and what is the average value?
a: SV = EDV - ESV (End-Diastolic Volume minus End-Systolic Volume). Average stroke volume: approximately 70 mL in a 70-kg person at rest.

q: What is CARDIAC OUTPUT, its formula, and average value?
a: Cardiac output (CO): volume of blood pumped by ONE VENTRICLE per minute. Formula: CO = Heart Rate (HR) x Stroke Volume (SV). Average: approximately 5 L/min at rest.

q: How does PARASYMPATHETIC stimulation affect heart rate, and what is the mechanism?
a: Parasympathetic stimulation DECREASES heart rate. Mechanism: ACh binds MUSCARINIC (M) receptors on autorhythmic cells. This INCREASES K+ permeability (K+ efflux hyperpolarizes the cell, so pacemaker potential starts at more negative value) AND DECREASES Ca2+ permeability (slows rate of depolarization). Both effects reduce firing frequency.

q: How does SYMPATHETIC stimulation affect heart rate, and what is the mechanism?
a: Sympathetic stimulation INCREASES heart rate. Mechanism: NE (norepinephrine) binds BETA-1 (B1) adrenergic receptors on autorhythmic cells. This INCREASES Na+ AND Ca2+ permeability, raising the rate of pacemaker depolarization. The membrane reaches threshold FASTER, increasing firing frequency.

q: Where is the cardiovascular control center located and what does it integrate?
a: The cardiovascular control center is located in the MEDULLA OBLONGATA. It is the central integrating center for ANTAGONISTIC autonomic control of heart rate (balancing parasympathetic and sympathetic inputs).

q: Under normal resting conditions, which division of the autonomic nervous system tonically DOMINATES heart rate control?
a: PARASYMPATHETIC tone normally DOMINATES heart rate control at rest. This is why the normal resting heart rate (~70 bpm) is below the SA node's intrinsic rate — the vagus nerve continuously slows the SA node.

q: What is CONTRACTILITY?
a: Contractility is the INTRINSIC ABILITY of a cardiac muscle fiber to CONTRACT at any given fiber length. It reflects the force of contraction independent of preload (fiber stretch). Increased contractility produces a greater stroke volume at the same EDV.

q: What is an INOTROPIC AGENT and give examples of positive and negative inotropes.
a: An inotropic agent is any chemical that affects CONTRACTILITY. POSITIVE inotropes (increase contractility): Epinephrine, Norepinephrine (catecholamines), Digitalis. NEGATIVE inotropes (decrease contractility): e.g., certain beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers.

q: What is the cellular mechanism by which catecholamines (epinephrine/norepinephrine) increase contractility? (Phospholamban pathway)
a: Catecholamines bind B1-receptors → activate cAMP second messenger system → phosphorylation of: (A) Voltage-gated Ca2+ channels: open time increases → more Ca2+ entry from ECF → more Ca2+ stored in SR. (B) Phospholamban (regulatory SR protein): increases Ca2+-ATPase activity → more Ca2+ pumped into SR → greater Ca2+ stores → more forceful contraction AND faster Ca2+ removal (shorter contraction duration).

q: What are the two outcomes of catecholamine-induced phosphorylation on cardiac contraction?
a: Outcome 1: MORE FORCEFUL CONTRACTION — increased Ca2+ stores in SR lead to greater Ca2+ release and stronger actin-myosin interaction. Outcome 2: SHORTER DURATION of contraction — Ca2+ is removed from the cytosol faster (shortens Ca2+-troponin binding time), allowing quicker relaxation.

q: What is the FRANK-STARLING LAW of the heart?
a: Frank-Starling Law: Stroke volume is PROPORTIONAL to EDV. Greater ventricular stretch (preload) at the end of diastole leads to a MORE FORCEFUL contraction and therefore a GREATER stroke volume. In essence: the heart pumps all the blood that is returned to it.

q: On the Starling Curve, what are the normal RESTING VALUES for EDV and SV?
a: Normal resting values (Starling curve): EDV approximately 135 mL; Stroke Volume approximately 70 mL. Cardiac muscle normally operates on the ASCENDING LIMB of its length-tension curve (unlike skeletal muscle, cardiac muscle does not reach the descending limb within the physiological range).

q: What is PRELOAD, what determines it, and which clinical conditions INCREASE it?
a: Preload: degree of ventricular STRETCH at the beginning of contraction, determined by EDV (which is determined by venous return). Greater preload → greater sarcomere stretch → more forceful contraction (Frank-Starling). Conditions that INCREASE preload: 1. Hypervolemia (increased blood volume). 2. Regurgitation of cardiac valves (backflow). 3. Heart failure.

q: What is AFTERLOAD, what determines it, and which clinical conditions INCREASE it?
a: Afterload: the combined load of EDV and ARTERIAL RESISTANCE that the ventricle must overcome during contraction. Determined by: EDV and arterial blood pressure. Conditions that INCREASE afterload: 1. Hypertension. 2. Vasoconstriction. Effect: Increased afterload = increased cardiac workload.

q: What is EJECTION FRACTION, its formula, and normal value range?
a: Ejection fraction: percentage of EDV that is EJECTED with each contraction. Formula: EF = Stroke Volume / EDV. Normal: 55-70% in healthy adults. Note: Some blood ALWAYS remains in the ventricle (ESV).

q: What are the clinical ejection fraction categories (HIGH, NORMAL, LOW, HEART FAILURE)?
a: HIGH function: Greater than 70%. NORMAL function: 55-70%. LOW function: 40-55%. POSSIBLE HEART FAILURE: Less than 40%.

q: What are the three main contributors to VENOUS RETURN?
a: 1. SKELETAL MUSCLE PUMP: skeletal muscle contractions during exercise squeeze veins, pushing blood toward the heart. 2. RESPIRATORY PUMP: pressure changes during breathing (especially inspiration) lower thoracic pressure, drawing blood toward the heart. 3. VENOUS CONSTRICTION: sympathetic stimulation constricts veins, reducing their capacity and increasing venous return.

q: What cardiac output factors are influenced by SYMPATHETIC stimulation / epinephrine?
a: Sympathetic/epinephrine increases: 1. HEART RATE (via increased Na+ and Ca2+ influx in pacemaker cells). 2. CONTRACTILITY (via B1 receptors and cAMP/phospholamban pathway). 3. VENOUS RETURN (via venous constriction, increasing EDV/preload).

q: What are the TWO TYPES of heart valves and their locations?
a: 1. ATRIOVENTRICULAR (AV) valves: located BETWEEN atria and ventricles — Tricuspid (right side), Bicuspid/Mitral (left side). 2. SEMILUNAR valves: located BETWEEN ventricles and arteries — Aortic valve (left ventricle to aorta), Pulmonary valve (right ventricle to pulmonary trunk). All valves ensure ONE-WAY FLOW.

q: What is the TRICUSPID VALVE — location and function?
a: The tricuspid valve is the RIGHT AV valve, located between the RIGHT ATRIUM and RIGHT VENTRICLE. It has three cusps. Function: ensures one-way blood flow from the right atrium into the right ventricle. Closure produces S1.

q: What is the BICUSPID (MITRAL) VALVE — location and function?
a: The bicuspid (mitral) valve is the LEFT AV valve, located between the LEFT ATRIUM and LEFT VENTRICLE. It has two cusps. Function: ensures one-way blood flow from the left atrium into the left ventricle. Closure produces S1.

q: What is the AORTIC SEMILUNAR VALVE — location and function?
a: The aortic valve is located between the LEFT VENTRICLE and the AORTA. Function: opens when left ventricular pressure exceeds aortic pressure (ventricular ejection); closes when ventricular pressure falls below aortic pressure (Phase 5), preventing backflow. Closure produces S2.

q: What is the PULMONARY SEMILUNAR VALVE — location and function?
a: The pulmonary valve is located between the RIGHT VENTRICLE and the PULMONARY TRUNK. Function: opens when right ventricular pressure exceeds pulmonary pressure (ejection); closes when pressure falls, preventing backflow of blood from the pulmonary circulation into the right ventricle. Closure contributes to S2.

q: What are CHORDAE TENDINEAE and PAPILLARY MUSCLES, and why are they important?
a: CHORDAE TENDINEAE: tendon-like cords that connect AV valve cusps to papillary muscles; they are taut during ventricular systole. PAPILLARY MUSCLES: muscles in the ventricular walls that CONTRACT simultaneously with ventricular systole, tightening the chordae tendineae. Together they PREVENT AV valve EVERSION (flipping back into the atria) during ventricular contraction.

q: What is the 3-step mechanism of AV VALVE OPENING?
a: Step 1: Blood returning to the heart fills the atria, increasing atrial pressure, which FORCES AV VALVES OPEN. Step 2: As ventricles fill, AV valve flaps HANG LIMPLY into the ventricles. Step 3: Atria contract (atrial systole), FORCING ADDITIONAL BLOOD into the ventricles.

q: What is the 3-step mechanism of AV VALVE CLOSING?
a: Step 1: Ventricles contract, FORCING BLOOD UPWARD AGAINST AV VALVE CUSPS. Step 2: AV VALVES CLOSE (atrial pressure is now less than ventricular pressure). Step 3: Papillary muscles contract and chordae tendineae TIGHTEN, PREVENTING valve flaps from EVERTING into the atria.

q: What is the mechanism of SEMILUNAR VALVE OPENING?
a: As ventricles contract and INTRAVENTRICULAR PRESSURE RISES, blood is pushed upward against the semilunar valves. When ventricular pressure EXCEEDS arterial pressure, blood FORCES THE SEMILUNAR VALVES OPEN and ejection begins.

q: What is the mechanism of SEMILUNAR VALVE CLOSING?
a: As ventricles relax and INTRAVENTRICULAR PRESSURE FALLS below arterial pressure, blood flows BACK from arteries toward the heart, FILLING THE CUSPS of the semilunar valves and FORCING THEM CLOSED. This backflow produces the dicrotic notch on the aortic pressure tracing.

q: What is a heart MURMUR and what causes it?
a: A heart murmur is an ABNORMAL heart sound. Normal blood flow is LAMINAR (smooth, streamlined) and SILENT. A murmur occurs when blood flow becomes TURBULENT — due to a structural valve problem — causing vibrations audible with a stethoscope.

q: What is an INCOMPETENT (insufficient/leaky) valve, and what sound does it produce?
a: An incompetent valve FAILS TO CLOSE COMPLETELY (insufficient seal). Result: TURBULENT BACKFLOW (regurgitation) through the leaky valve. Sound: A SWISHING sound. Also called an insufficient or leaky valve.

q: What is a STENOTIC valve, and what sound does it produce?
a: A stenotic valve is STIFFENED AND NARROWED, failing to open fully. Result: TURBULENT FLOW through the narrowed opening. Sound: A HIGH-PITCHED WHISTLE or a CLICK. The narrowed orifice forces blood through at high velocity.

q: Murmur pattern 'Lub – SWISH – Dup': what does this indicate?
a: Lub-SWISH-Dup = SYSTOLIC murmur between S1 and S2. Indicates: INSUFFICIENT (leaky) AV valve (mitral or tricuspid). During systole, when the AV valve should be closed, blood leaks backward through the incompetent valve, causing turbulent backflow (the swish).

q: Murmur pattern 'Lub – Dup – SWISH': what does this indicate?
a: Lub-Dup-SWISH = DIASTOLIC murmur after S2. Indicates: INSUFFICIENT (leaky) SEMILUNAR valve (pulmonary or aortic). During diastole, when the semilunar valve should be closed, blood leaks backward from the artery into the ventricle (the swish).

q: Murmur pattern 'Lub – WHISTLE – Dup': what does this indicate?
a: Lub-WHISTLE-Dup = SYSTOLIC murmur between S1 and S2. Indicates: STENOTIC SEMILUNAR valve (pulmonary or aortic). During systole, blood is forced through a narrowed semilunar valve opening, producing a high-pitched whistle.

q: Murmur pattern 'Lub – Dup – WHISTLE': what does this indicate?
a: Lub-Dup-WHISTLE = DIASTOLIC murmur after S2. Indicates: STENOTIC AV valve (mitral or tricuspid stenosis). During diastole, when blood is filling the ventricle through the AV valve, the narrowed AV valve opening forces blood through at high velocity, producing the whistle.

q: Compare normal valve sounds vs. abnormal valve sounds (4-panel summary).
a: Normal OPEN valve: laminar flow = QUIET. Normal CLOSED valve: no flow = QUIET. Stenotic (narrowed) valve: turbulent flow through narrow opening = MURMUR. Insufficient (leaky) valve: turbulent backflow = MURMUR.

q: What is DIGITALIS and how does it function as a cardiac drug?
a: Digitalis is a POSITIVE INOTROPE — it INCREASES cardiac contractility. It works by inhibiting the Na+/K+-ATPase pump, raising intracellular Na+, which then reduces Ca2+ efflux via the NCX exchanger, resulting in MORE Ca2+ available for contraction (greater force).

q: What are the four functional roles of the major vessel types in the cardiovascular system? (Ch. 15 preview)
a: 1. ELASTIC SYSTEMIC ARTERIES: pressure reservoir that maintains blood flow during ventricular relaxation. 2. ARTERIOLES: site of variable resistance (adjustable diameter controls blood distribution). 3. CAPILLARIES: site of exchange between blood and cells. 4. SYSTEMIC VEINS: expandable volume reservoir (low-pressure reservoir).

q: Table 14.3: What events lead to THRESHOLD in each of the three muscle types?
a: Skeletal muscle: Net Na+ entry through ACh-operated (nicotinic) channels at neuromuscular junction. Contractile myocardium: Depolarization ENTERS via gap junctions from adjacent cells. Autorhythmic myocardium: Net Na+ entry through If channels; REINFORCED by Ca2+ entry near threshold.

q: Table 14.3: Does HYPERPOLARIZATION occur in contractile myocardium after repolarization?
a: NO hyperpolarization in contractile myocardium. The resting potential is -90 mV, which is at (or near) the equilibrium potential for K+, so no overshoot occurs. Contrast with skeletal muscle (brief hyperpolarization due to excessive K+ efflux) and autorhythmic cells (normally none, but ACh CAN hyperpolarize autorhythmic cells).

q: How does an ECG DIFFER from a single-cell action potential recording?
a: ECG: records SUMMED electrical activity of ALL cardiac cells from BODY SURFACE electrodes — smaller amplitude (~1 mV), can detect both depolarization and repolarization events as separate waves. Single-cell AP: intracellular recording — much LARGER amplitude (~110 mV), recorded directly at the source. The ECG is NOT simply a single AP.

q: What factors affect HEART RATE (HR) as a determinant of cardiac output?
a: HR is determined by the RATE OF DEPOLARIZATION in autorhythmic cells. It is DECREASED by parasympathetic innervation (ACh on M receptors). It is INCREASED by sympathetic innervation (NE on B1 receptors) and epinephrine.

q: What factors affect STROKE VOLUME (SV) as a determinant of cardiac output?
a: SV is determined by FORCE of contraction and is influenced by: 1. CONTRACTILITY (increased by sympathetic/epinephrine via cAMP/Ca2+). 2. END-DIASTOLIC VOLUME (preload) — determined by venous return, which is aided by skeletal muscle pump, respiratory pump, and venous constriction.

q: END
a: END

q: What is the primary function of the cardiovascular system?
a: Transport of materials to and from all parts of the body — including O2, nutrients, hormones, wastes, immune cells, and heat.

q: What three things make up the basic structure of the cardiovascular system (in simplest terms)?
a: A series of tubes (blood vessels) filled with fluid (blood) connected to a pump (the heart).

q: Name the two materials that ENTER the body via the cardiovascular system and their sources.
a: (1) Oxygen: from lungs to all cells. (2) Nutrients and water: from intestinal tract to all cells.

q: Name four categories of materials moved CELL-TO-CELL via the cardiovascular system.
a: (1) Metabolic wastes (cells to liver). (2) Immune cells, antibodies, clotting proteins (circulate continuously). (3) Hormones (endocrine cells to target cells). (4) Stored nutrients — glucose, fatty acids (liver/adipose to all cells).

q: Name the three materials that LEAVE the body via the cardiovascular system and their destinations.
a: (1) Metabolic wastes: all cells to kidneys. (2) Heat: all cells to skin. (3) Carbon dioxide: all cells to lungs.

q: What is hypoxia, and why is the brain especially sensitive to it?
a: Hypoxia = a low-oxygen state. Brain neurons have very high O2 consumption and cannot meet ATP needs via anaerobic pathways. Loss of consciousness occurs 5–10 sec after blood flow stops; permanent brain damage occurs after 5–10 min without O2.

q: What is cyanosis and what causes it?
a: Cyanosis = a bluish discoloration of the skin (e.g., around mouth, under fingernails) caused by low-oxygen blood. In living tissue, well-oxygenated blood is bright red; low-oxygen blood is darker red — but drawings use blue by convention.

q: Define: artery vs. vein.
a: Artery: a blood vessel that carries blood AWAY from the heart. Vein: a blood vessel that RETURNS blood TO the heart.

q: What is the septum, and what does it divide in the heart?
a: The septum is a central wall that divides the heart into left and right halves, each functioning as an independent pump. Blood on each side does not mix.

q: What is the function of the RIGHT side of the heart?
a: Receives deoxygenated blood returning from the tissues and pumps it to the lungs for oxygenation (via pulmonary arteries).

q: What is the function of the LEFT side of the heart?
a: Receives freshly oxygenated blood from the lungs (via pulmonary veins) and pumps it to tissues throughout the body (via the aorta).

q: Define pulmonary circulation.
a: The blood vessels carrying blood from the right ventricle to the lungs and back to the left atrium — includes pulmonary arteries (right ventricle to lungs) and pulmonary veins (lungs to left atrium).

q: Define systemic circulation.
a: The blood vessels carrying blood from the left side of the heart to all tissues of the body (except lungs) and returning it to the right side of the heart.

q: Trace blood flow starting from the RIGHT ATRIUM through both circulations back to the right atrium.
a: Right atrium → right ventricle → pulmonary arteries → lungs (oxygenation) → pulmonary veins → left atrium → left ventricle → aorta → systemic arteries → capillaries → veins → superior/inferior vena cava → right atrium.

q: What is a portal system? Name the three portal systems of the body.
a: A portal system = two capillary beds joined in series. (1) Hepatic portal system: digestive tract capillaries → hepatic portal vein → liver capillaries. (2) Renal portal system: two capillary beds in kidneys. (3) Hypothalamic-hypophyseal portal system: hypothalamus to anterior pituitary.

q: What did William Harvey prove about blood circulation, and why was it significant?
a: Harvey calculated that blood pumped per hour exceeds total body weight — proving the liver could not continuously make new blood. He showed valves in the heart and veins create one-way flow, veins carry blood TO the heart, and blood recirculates rather than being consumed. He founded modern cardiovascular physiology.

q: Who discovered capillaries, and when? What is the etymology of 'capillary'?
a: Marcello Malpighi, Italian anatomist, mid-17th century — observed them microscopically. Etymology: capillus = hair (microscopic, hair-like vessels).

q: What does MYOGENIC mean in reference to the heart?
a: Myogenic = originating within the heart muscle itself. The heart can contract without any outside neural signal because its contraction signal arises from specialized autorhythmic cells within the myocardium.

q: Write the fundamental equation for blood flow (Q). What does each variable represent?
a: Q = deltaP / R. Q = flow rate (mL/min); deltaP = pressure gradient (difference in pressure between two points); R = resistance to flow.

q: How does vessel RADIUS affect resistance to blood flow? (Poiseuille's Law)
a: Resistance is inversely proportional to the FOURTH power of the radius (R is proportional to 1/r^4). A small decrease in radius causes a large increase in resistance. Example: halving the radius increases resistance 16-fold.

q: What is the equation for velocity of flow (v)? How does it differ from flow rate (Q)?
a: v = Q / A (velocity = flow rate divided by cross-sectional area). Flow rate (Q) = total volume per unit time through a cross section; velocity (v) = speed of movement. In a tube of variable diameter, velocity varies INVERSELY with cross-sectional area — faster in narrow sections, slower in wider sections.

q: What is the equation for Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP)?
a: MAP is proportional to cardiac output (CO) x peripheral resistance. MAP is the primary driving force for blood flow and is influenced by: (1) cardiac output and (2) peripheral resistance of blood vessels.

q: Define ischemia. How does partial arterial blockage affect resistance and velocity?
a: Ischemia = decreased blood supply to tissue (from Greek: ischein = to suppress + -emia = blood). Partial blockage: decreased radius → resistance INCREASES (R proportional to 1/r^4). If flow (Q) stays the same but cross-sectional area decreases → velocity INCREASES (v = Q/A).

q: Describe the SIZE and LOCATION of the heart.
a: Approximately the size of a fist. Located in the CENTER of the thoracic cavity, on the ventral side, sandwiched between the two lungs, with the apex resting on the diaphragm.

q: Describe the APEX and BASE of the heart.
a: Apex = pointed end, angles down to the LEFT side of the body, rests on the diaphragm. Base = broader end, lies just behind the sternum (breastbone), and is where all major blood vessels attach. Memory aid: like an inverted cone with apex down and base up.

q: Define pericardium. What does pericardial fluid do?
a: Pericardium (peri = around + kardia = heart) = tough membranous sac encasing the heart. A thin layer of clear pericardial fluid inside lubricates the external surface of the heart as it beats.

q: What is pericarditis? What is a friction rub?
a: Pericarditis = inflammation of the pericardium. It may reduce lubrication, causing the heart to rub against the pericardium — producing an abnormal heart sound called a friction rub.

q: Define myocardium. What is it composed of, and what layer is the thickest?
a: Myocardium (myo = muscle + kardia = heart) = cardiac muscle making up most of the heart. Covered by thin outer/inner layers of epithelium and connective tissue. The thick muscular walls of the VENTRICLES form the bulk of the heart. Atria are thinner-walled and lie above the ventricles.

q: What are the four fibrous connective tissue rings in the heart? What are their two key functions?
a: Four fibrous rings surround the four heart valves. Functions: (1) They form the origin and insertion for cardiac muscle — their arrangement pulls apex and base together during ventricular contraction. (2) They act as electrical insulators, blocking direct transmission of electrical signals from atria to ventricles, forcing signals through the specialized conduction system.

q: What is the pulmonary trunk?
a: The pulmonary trunk (also called pulmonary artery) is the large vessel that carries blood from the RIGHT VENTRICLE to the lungs. It branches into left and right pulmonary arteries.

q: What vessels return blood TO the heart? Distinguish superior vena cava from inferior vena cava.
a: Superior vena cava: returns deoxygenated blood from the upper body (head, arms, upper trunk) to the right atrium. Inferior vena cava: returns deoxygenated blood from the lower body (abdomen, pelvis, legs) to the right atrium. Pulmonary veins return oxygenated blood from the lungs to the left atrium.

q: What are the two sets of heart valves? What is the general function of both sets?
a: (1) Atrioventricular (AV) valves: between atria and ventricles. (2) Semilunar valves: between ventricles and arteries (named for crescent-moon shape). FUNCTION of both: prevent backward flow of blood, ensuring one-way circulation.

q: Describe the TRICUSPID VALVE: location, number of flaps, memory aid.
a: Located between the RIGHT atrium and RIGHT ventricle. Has THREE flaps (cusps). Memory aid: R-S-T = Right Side has the Tricuspid.

q: Describe the BICUSPID (MITRAL) VALVE: location, number of flaps, alternate name origin.
a: Located between the LEFT atrium and LEFT ventricle. Has TWO flaps. Also called the MITRAL valve — resembles the tall headdress (miter) worn by popes and bishops.

q: What are chordae tendineae and papillary muscles? What is their function?
a: Chordae tendineae = collagenous tendons connecting the edges of AV valve flaps to papillary muscles on the ventricular wall. Papillary muscles = mound-like extensions of ventricular muscle. FUNCTION: provide stability for the chordae; prevent AV valves from being pushed backward into the atrium during ventricular contraction (like umbrella struts). They cannot actively open/close the valves — valves move PASSIVELY.

q: What is valve PROLAPSE and what causes it?
a: Prolapse = when the AV valve is pushed backward (prolapses) into the atrium during ventricular contraction. Caused by FAILURE of the chordae tendineae — without the chordae tethering the valve flaps, ventricular pressure can force the valve the wrong way.

q: Describe the AORTIC and PULMONARY (semilunar) valves: location, structure, and why they do NOT need chordae.
a: Aortic valve: between LEFT ventricle and the aorta. Pulmonary valve: between RIGHT ventricle and pulmonary trunk. Each has THREE cuplike leaflets. They do NOT need chordae tendineae because their cuplike shape allows backflowing blood to fill the cups and snap them closed — no tendons required.

q: Define the coronary circulation. Where do the coronary arteries originate?
a: The coronary circulation is the heart's own special blood supply. Coronary (corona = crown) refers to vessels that encircle the heart near its base. The two primary coronary arteries originate at the ROOT of the AORTA (start of the aorta), just superior to the semilunar valve leaflets of the aortic valve.

q: What regions does the RIGHT CORONARY ARTERY (RCA) supply?
a: The RCA runs around the right side of the heart in the coronary sulcus. It supplies: right atrium, most of the right ventricle, some of the left ventricle, and the POSTERIOR portion of the interventricular septum.

q: What are the two main branches of the LEFT CORONARY ARTERY (LCA) and what do they supply?
a: (1) CIRCUMFLEX branch: continues around the left side of the heart to the posterior surface — supplies left atrium and posterior left ventricle. (2) ANTERIOR INTERVENTRICULAR branch (clinically: LAD = Left Anterior Descending): runs toward the apex — supplies most of the left ventricle, interventricular septum, and some of the right ventricle.

q: How is coronary venous blood drained? Name all three routes.
a: (1) ~85% drains through cardiac veins into the CORONARY SINUS (on posterior heart) → directly into the right atrium. (2) Small blood channels deep in heart muscle drain directly into the heart's chambers. (3) A few small veins on the anterior right ventricle drain directly into the right atrium.

q: What percentage of delivered oxygen does cardiac muscle extract? Why is this clinically significant?
a: Cardiac muscle consumes 70–80% of delivered O2 — more than TWICE the amount extracted by other cells. During increased activity, the heart uses almost ALL delivered O2. Therefore, the ONLY way to get more O2 to an exercising heart is to INCREASE blood flow — reduced coronary flow (e.g., from blockage) can quickly damage or kill heart muscle.

q: What are the TWO types of myocardial cells? What percentage of cells are autorhythmic?
a: (1) AUTORHYTHMIC cells (pacemakers): generate action potentials spontaneously; ~1% of myocardial cells. (2) CONTRACTILE cells: typical striated muscle with sarcomeres; form the bulk of the heart and generate the force of contraction.

q: What are the structural features of AUTORHYTHMIC (pacemaker) cells that distinguish them from contractile cells?
a: Autorhythmic cells are smaller, contain FEW contractile fibers, do NOT have organized sarcomeres, and do NOT contribute to the contractile force of the heart. Their function is to generate spontaneous action potentials (not to contract).

q: List SIX structural differences between cardiac contractile cells and skeletal muscle fibers.
a: (1) SIZE: cardiac cells are much SMALLER; usually have a SINGLE nucleus. (2) BRANCHING: cardiac cells branch and join end-to-end to form a network; skeletal cells do not branch. (3) INTERCALATED DISKS: unique junctions with desmosomes and gap junctions. (4) T-TUBULES: larger and branched in cardiac cells. (5) SR: smaller in cardiac cells (depends on ECF Ca2+). (6) MITOCHONDRIA: occupy ~1/3 of cardiac cell volume (high energy demand).

q: What are intercalated disks? What are their two components and what does each do?
a: Intercalated disks = specialized junctions between cardiac muscle cells consisting of interdigitated membranes. (1) DESMOSOMES: strong connections that tie adjacent cells together and transfer force from cell to cell. (2) GAP JUNCTIONS: electrically connect cells, allowing waves of depolarization to spread rapidly so all heart muscle cells contract almost simultaneously.

q: What is Ca2+-induced Ca2+ release (CICR)? Which channel is responsible?
a: CICR: when a small amount of Ca2+ enters from the ECF through voltage-gated Ca2+ channels during the action potential, it triggers RYANODINE RECEPTOR (RyR) channels on the sarcoplasmic reticulum to open and release a much larger amount of stored Ca2+ into the cytosol. Ca2+ entry triggers Ca2+ release — the entering Ca2+ is the trigger.

q: Describe the 10 steps of EC coupling in a cardiac contractile cell (contraction steps 1–6).
a: 1. Action potential enters from adjacent cell via gap junctions. 2. Depolarization spreads through T-tubules into cell interior. 3. Voltage-gated Ca2+ channels on T-tubule membrane open; Ca2+ enters from ECF. 4. Ca2+ triggers RyR channels on SR to open — stored Ca2+ flows into cytosol (Ca2+ spark). 5. Multiple sparks sum to create a Ca2+ signal. 6. Ca2+ binds to troponin → crossbridges form → sliding filament contraction.

q: Describe the relaxation steps (steps 7–10) in cardiac EC coupling. What proteins are involved?
a: 7. Ca2+ unbinds from troponin → myosin releases actin → filaments return to relaxed position. 8. Ca2+ pumped back into SR via Ca2+-ATPase. 9. Remaining cytosolic Ca2+ removed via Na+-Ca2+ exchanger (NCX antiporter) — 1 Ca2+ out in exchange for 3 Na2+ in. 10. Na+ that entered via NCX is removed by Na+-K+-ATPase to maintain the Na+ gradient.

q: What are the relative contributions of SR vs. ECF to the Ca2+ used for cardiac muscle contraction?
a: SR provides ~90% of Ca2+ needed for contraction. The remaining ~10% enters from the extracellular fluid through voltage-gated Ca2+ channels. This differs from skeletal muscle, which depends entirely on SR Ca2+ and does NOT require extracellular Ca2+.

q: What is the Na+-Ca2+ exchanger (NCX)? What is its stoichiometry and energy source?
a: NCX = antiporter that removes Ca2+ from the cardiac cell. It moves 1 Ca2+ OUT of the cell against its electrochemical gradient in exchange for 3 Na+ IN (down their gradient). Energy = the Na+ electrochemical gradient (indirect active transport), which is maintained by Na+-K+-ATPase.

q: How can cardiac muscle generate GRADED contractions? (This contrasts with what property of skeletal muscle?)
a: A single cardiac muscle fiber can vary the amount of force it generates — GRADED contraction. Force is proportional to the number of active crossbridges, which depends on how much Ca2+ is bound to troponin. More Ca2+ entering from ECF → more CICR → more crossbridges → more force. This contrasts with skeletal muscle, where a single fiber contracts in an ALL-OR-NONE fashion at any given length.

q: What is the RESTING MEMBRANE POTENTIAL of a cardiac contractile cell, and what phase is it called?
a: Phase 4: stable resting membrane potential of approximately -90 mV. This is maintained by the equilibrium potential for K+ (K+ leak channels dominate at rest).

q: Describe Phase 0 (DEPOLARIZATION) of the contractile cell action potential.
a: Phase 0: A wave of depolarization enters from adjacent cells through gap junctions. Voltage-gated Na+ channels open rapidly → rapid Na+ influx → membrane potential shoots from -90 mV to about +20 mV. Channels are double-gated (similar to axon Na+ channels).

q: Describe Phase 1 (INITIAL REPOLARIZATION) of the contractile cell action potential.
a: Phase 1: Na+ channels close. The cell begins to repolarize as K+ leaves through open (fast) K+ channels. This initial repolarization is very brief.

q: Describe Phase 2 (PLATEAU) of the contractile cell action potential. What two events cause the plateau?
a: Phase 2: The action potential flattens into a plateau due to: (1) DECREASE in K+ permeability — fast K+ channels close. (2) INCREASE in Ca2+ permeability — voltage-gated Ca2+ channels slowly open during phases 0-1 and now allow Ca2+ influx. Ca2+ influx + decreased K+ efflux balance each other → membrane potential stays flat (plateau).

q: Describe Phase 3 (RAPID REPOLARIZATION) of the contractile cell action potential.
a: Phase 3: Ca2+ channels close AND slow K+ channels open (these K+ channels were activated by depolarization but slow to respond). K+ exits rapidly → cell returns to resting potential (Phase 4).

q: How long does a cardiac contractile cell action potential last compared to a neuronal AP?
a: Neuronal (or skeletal muscle) AP: 1–5 msec. Cardiac contractile cell AP: 200 msec or more. The prolonged duration is due to Ca2+ entry during the plateau phase (Phase 2).

q: What is the REFRACTORY PERIOD in cardiac muscle? Why is it important?
a: Refractory period = time following an AP during which a normal stimulus cannot trigger a second AP. In cardiac muscle, the long AP (200+ msec) means the refractory period lasts almost as long as the entire muscle twitch. By the time a second AP can fire, the cell has almost completely RELAXED. This PREVENTS SUMMATION and thus PREVENTS TETANUS.

q: Why can cardiac muscle NOT undergo tetanus, and why is this physiologically critical?
a: Tetanus cannot occur because the long refractory period prevents summation — each contraction must complete before another AP can trigger. This is CRITICAL because tetanus (sustained contraction) would prevent the ventricles from RELAXING and filling with blood between beats — the heart would stop pumping effectively and be fatal.

q: What is a PACEMAKER POTENTIAL, and what makes it unique compared to a resting membrane potential?
a: Pacemaker potential = the unstable membrane potential of autorhythmic cells that starts at approximately -60 mV and slowly drifts UPWARD toward threshold (never rests at a constant value). When it reaches threshold, the cell fires an AP. Unlike a resting MP (stable), the pacemaker potential is always changing — hence pacemaker cells fire SPONTANEOUSLY.

q: What are IF CHANNELS (funny current channels)? What is the full name, and why are they called 'funny'?
a: If channels = ion channels that open at NEGATIVE membrane potentials (around -60 mV) in autorhythmic cells. Full name: HCN channels (Hyperpolarization-Activated Cyclic Nucleotide-Gated channels). Called 'funny' because researchers initially did not understand their unusual behavior of opening at hyperpolarized (negative) potentials, opposite of most voltage-gated channels.

q: What ions flow through If channels, and what is the net result?
a: If channels are permeable to BOTH K+ and Na+. When they open at -60 mV, Na+ INFLUX exceeds K+ efflux → net influx of positive charge → slowly DEPOLARIZES the autorhythmic cell (drives the pacemaker potential toward threshold).

q: Describe the full sequence of events in the pacemaker potential and autorhythmic cell action potential (phases with ion channels).
a: 1. At -60 mV: If channels OPEN → net Na+ influx → slow depolarization. 2. As potential becomes more positive: If channels gradually CLOSE; one set of Ca2+ channels OPENS. 3. Ca2+ influx continues depolarization toward threshold. 4. At threshold: different subtype of voltage-gated Ca2+ channels open → Ca2+ influx → STEEP DEPOLARIZATION (not Na+, unlike other cells). 5. Repolarization: Ca2+ channels close; slow K+ channels open → K+ efflux → repolarization back to -60 mV → If channels reopen.

q: Compare the RESTING MEMBRANE POTENTIAL of the three cardiac/muscle cell types (skeletal, contractile myocardium, autorhythmic myocardium).
a: Skeletal muscle: stable at -70 to -80 mV. Contractile myocardium: stable at -90 mV. Autorhythmic myocardium: UNSTABLE pacemaker potential, usually starts at -60 mV and drifts upward.

q: Compare the RISING PHASE of the action potential in the three cell types.
a: Skeletal muscle: Na+ entry (through ACh-activated channels → AP via voltage-gated Na+ channels). Contractile myocardium: Na+ entry (voltage-gated Na+ channels — same rapid depolarization). Autorhythmic myocardium: Ca2+ entry (voltage-gated Ca2+ channels — NOT Na+ channels).

q: Compare the DURATION of action potentials in the three cell types.
a: Skeletal muscle: SHORT — 1–2 msec. Contractile myocardium: EXTENDED — 200+ msec (due to Ca2+ plateau phase). Autorhythmic myocardium: variable, generally 150+ msec.

q: How does depolarization reach the contractile myocardium (events leading to threshold) compared to skeletal muscle?
a: Skeletal muscle: net Na+ entry through ACh-operated channels (at NMJ). Contractile myocardium: depolarization enters via GAP JUNCTIONS from autorhythmic or adjacent contractile cells. Autorhythmic myocardium: net Na+ entry through If channels, reinforced by Ca2+ entry.

q: What is the SINOATRIAL (SA) NODE? Where is it located and what is its intrinsic rate?
a: The SA node is a cluster of autorhythmic cells in the RIGHT ATRIUM that serves as the MAIN PACEMAKER of the heart. Intrinsic rate: ~70 beats per minute (fastest of all pacemakers, so it sets the heart rate).

q: What is the ATRIOVENTRICULAR (AV) NODE? Where is it located and what is its backup rate?
a: The AV node is a group of autorhythmic cells located at the FLOOR of the RIGHT ATRIUM. Backup pacemaker rate: ~50 beats per minute. Primary function: acts as the ONLY electrical pathway through which signals can reach the ventricles from the atria.

q: Describe the 5-step conduction sequence through the heart, including all structures.
a: 1. SA node depolarizes (right atrium). 2. Signal travels rapidly to AV node via INTERNODAL PATHWAYS; depolarization also spreads across atria. 3. Conduction slows through AV node (AV NODE DELAY). 4. From AV node: signal travels rapidly through AV BUNDLE (bundle of His) in ventricular septum → left and right BUNDLE BRANCHES → PURKINJE FIBERS to the APEX. 5. Depolarization wave spreads UPWARD from apex through contractile cells.

q: What are Purkinje fibers? What is their conduction speed and intrinsic rate?
a: Purkinje fibers = specialized conducting cells of the ventricles that transmit electrical signals very rapidly. Conduction speed: up to 4 m/sec (ensures nearly simultaneous contraction of all apical contractile cells). Intrinsic pacemaker rate if they take over: ~25–40 beats per minute.

q: What is AV NODE DELAY? How fast does conduction move through the AV node vs. the internodal pathway?
a: AV node delay = the AV node SLOWS transmission of action potentials to allow the ATRIA to COMPLETE their contraction before ventricular contraction begins. AV node conduction is only 1/20 the rate of action potentials in the atrial internodal pathway.

q: Why must ventricular contraction proceed from APEX to BASE? What happens if the signal travels top-down instead?
a: Blood exits ventricles through openings at the TOP (base). Apex-to-base contraction squeezes blood UPWARD toward the arterial exits. If signals traveled top-down (base-to-apex): ventricles would start contracting at the top and blood would be pushed DOWNWARD, trapped at the bottom — like squeezing a toothpaste tube from the top.

q: What is the HIERARCHY of pacemakers and why does the fastest pacemaker determine heart rate?
a: SA node (~70 bpm) > AV node (~50 bpm) > Purkinje fibers (~35 bpm). The fastest pacemaker sets the rate because all other autorhythmic and contractile cells follow it (like following the fastest walker). Slower pacemakers never get a chance to fire because they are always depolarized by the faster SA node signal first.

q: What is COMPLETE HEART BLOCK? What happens to heart rate and what treatment is required?
a: Complete heart block = disruption of electrical signal conduction from atria to ventricles through the AV node. SA node fires normally (~70 bpm) but signals NEVER REACH the ventricles. Ventricles revert to their intrinsic Purkinje fiber rate (~35 bpm). Rate too slow to maintain adequate blood flow → may require a surgically implanted MECHANICAL PACEMAKER (battery-powered device).

q: What is an ECTOPIC PACEMAKER? What happens if one fires faster than the SA node?
a: Ectopic pacemaker = an abnormal pacemaker that develops out of place in the heart's conducting system (ektopos = out of place). If an ectopic pacemaker fires faster than the SA node, it TAKES OVER as the dominant pacemaker and increases the heart rate to its faster rate.

q: Who is the 'father of the modern ECG'? What are his contributions?
a: Walter Einthoven, Dutch physiologist. Contributions: (1) Named the parts of the ECG as we know them. (2) Created 'Einthoven's triangle' — a hypothetical triangle around the heart using electrodes on both arms and left leg, with sides numbered to correspond to three LEADS (pairs of electrodes). First human ECG recorded in 1887; refined for clinical use in early 20th century.

q: What does an ECG record? How does it differ from an intracellular action potential recording?
a: ECG = records the SUMMED electrical activity of ALL cells of the heart from electrodes on the body surface (extracellular). Amplitude: only ~1 mV at body surface. An intracellular action potential records ONE cell with an intracellular electrode — amplitude ~110 mV and upward deflection always means depolarization. ECG direction of deflection depends only on direction of current flow relative to the lead axis.

q: What does the P WAVE represent on an ECG?
a: P wave = atrial DEPOLARIZATION. Atrial contraction begins during the latter part of the P wave.

q: What does the QRS COMPLEX represent on an ECG? What are the Q, R, and S waves individually?
a: QRS complex = progressive wave of VENTRICULAR DEPOLARIZATION. Q wave = beginning of ventricular depolarization; R wave = peak of ventricular depolarization; S wave = end of ventricular depolarization. (Q wave is sometimes absent on normal ECGs.) Ventricular contraction begins just AFTER the Q wave.

q: What does the T WAVE represent? Why is there NO separate wave for atrial repolarization?
a: T wave = ventricular REPOLARIZATION (ventricles relaxing). Atrial repolarization has no separate wave because it is INCORPORATED INTO and MASKED BY the QRS complex — it occurs simultaneously with ventricular depolarization.

q: What are the P-R SEGMENT and PR INTERVAL? What event occurs during each?
a: P-R segment = the baseline section between the P wave and QRS complex — represents conduction through the AV node and AV bundle (AV node delay). PR interval = P wave + P-R segment combined. A longer-than-normal PR interval suggests slowed AV node conduction (e.g., heart block).

q: What are the S-T SEGMENT and QT INTERVAL?
a: S-T segment = baseline section between the QRS complex and T wave — represents early ventricular repolarization; clinically important (S-T elevation or depression can indicate MI or ischemia). QT interval = QRS complex + S-T segment + T wave combined — represents total duration of ventricular electrical activity.

q: Complete the ECG-to-mechanical event table: P wave → ?, P-R segment → ?, QRS complex → ?, within QRS → ?, T wave → ?, T-P segment → ?
a: P wave → atrial contraction begins. P-R segment → atrial contraction continues; ventricular filling. QRS complex → ventricular contraction begins. Within QRS → atrial RELAXATION (atrial repolarization masked by QRS). T wave → ventricular RELAXATION begins. T-P segment → both atria and ventricles electrically quiet and relaxed.

q: What is TACHYCARDIA? BRADYCARDIA? ARRHYTHMIA? What is the normal resting heart rate range?
a: Normal resting HR: 60–100 bpm. Tachycardia (tachys = swift): faster-than-normal heart rate (>100 bpm). Bradycardia (bradys = slow): slower-than-normal heart rate (<60 bpm). Arrhythmia (a- = without + rhythm): any irregular heart rhythm.

q: What are PREMATURE VENTRICULAR CONTRACTIONS (PVCs)?
a: PVCs = extra beats occurring when an autorhythmic cell OTHER THAN the SA node fires an action potential out of sequence — an ectopic pacemaker in the ventricle. Classified as cardiac arrhythmias.

q: What is LONG QT SYNDROME (LQTS)? Name three forms.
a: LQTS = prolonged QT interval on ECG, indicating extended ventricular electrical activity — dangerous risk of fatal arrhythmia. Forms: (1) Inherited channelopathies — mutations in myocardial Na+ or K+ channels. (2) Defective ankyrin-B protein (anchors ion channels to membrane — channels normal but misanchored). (3) Iatrogenic LQTS — drug side effect (e.g., terfenadine/Seldane blocking K+ repolarization channels; removed from market after 8+ deaths).

q: Distinguish ATRIAL FIBRILLATION from VENTRICULAR FIBRILLATION. How is ventricular fibrillation treated?
a: Atrial fibrillation: common condition, often asymptomatic, but can lead to stroke if untreated. Ventricular fibrillation: IMMEDIATELY LIFE-THREATENING — no coordinated contraction, ventricles cannot pump blood, brain loses O2. Treatment: electrical DEFIBRILLATION — shock creates simultaneous depolarization of ALL cardiac cells, resynchronizing them. (ECG shows completely chaotic, irregular waveforms with no recognizable P, QRS, or T waves.)

q: Describe PHASE 1 of the cardiac cycle (Late Diastole). What valves are open/closed?
a: Phase 1 — Late Diastole: Both atria and ventricles are RELAXED. Blood flows PASSIVELY by gravity from atria into ventricles (ventricles expand to accommodate). AV valves: OPEN. Semilunar valves: CLOSED. This is the starting point of the cardiac cycle.

q: Describe PHASE 2 of the cardiac cycle (Atrial Systole). What percentage of ventricular filling occurs here?
a: Phase 2 — Atrial Systole: Atria contract, forcing the LAST ~20% of blood into the ventricles (most ventricular filling occurs passively during Phase 1). The depolarization wave sweeps across the atria. During exercise/high heart rate, atrial contraction plays a GREATER role in ventricular filling. At the END of atrial systole = MAXIMUM blood in ventricles = END-DIASTOLIC VOLUME (EDV).

q: Describe PHASE 3 of the cardiac cycle (Isovolumic Ventricular Contraction). Define EDV. What is the first heart sound?
a: Phase 3 — Isovolumic Ventricular Contraction: Depolarization reaches the apex; ventricular systole begins; blood pushes AV valves CLOSED (S1). Both AV AND semilunar valves are closed — blood has nowhere to go. Pressure increases rapidly but volume does NOT change (iso = equal, volumic = volume). EDV = end-diastolic volume = maximum blood volume in ventricle (~135 mL at rest). S1 (first heart sound, 'lub') = caused by CLOSURE of AV valves.

q: Describe PHASE 4 of the cardiac cycle (Ventricular Ejection). What is ESV?
a: Phase 4 — Ventricular Ejection: Ventricular pressure exceeds arterial pressure → semilunar valves OPEN → blood ejected into arteries. AV valves remain CLOSED; atria continue to fill. Heart does NOT empty completely — volume remaining at END of contraction = ESV (end-systolic volume, ~65 mL at rest). High-pressure blood displaces low-pressure arterial blood, pushing it farther into vasculature.

q: Describe PHASE 5 of the cardiac cycle (Isovolumic Ventricular Relaxation). What is the second heart sound?
a: Phase 5 — Isovolumic Ventricular Relaxation: Ventricles repolarize and relax; ventricular pressure falls. When ventricular pressure drops below ARTERIAL pressure → blood starts flowing backward → fills cuplike semilunar valve cusps → SNAPS them CLOSED → S2 (second heart sound, 'dup'). Both sets of valves closed again; volume does not change. When ventricular pressure finally falls below ATRIAL pressure → AV valves open → cycle begins again.

q: Summarize ALL FOUR heart sounds: S1, S2, S3, S4 — cause and clinical relevance.
a: S1 ('lub'): AV valve CLOSURE — beginning of ventricular systole. S2 ('dup'): Semilunar valve CLOSURE — beginning of isovolumic relaxation. S3: Turbulent blood flow into ventricles during ventricular FILLING. S4: Turbulence during ATRIAL contraction. S3 and S4 are normally inaudible through a stethoscope; when audible they are called GALLOPS (heard as: 'lub-lub-dup' or 'lub-dup-dup'). Clicks arise from abnormal valve movement. Murmurs from stenotic or incompetent valves.

q: What are MURMURS? What are their two causes? Define valvular stenosis.
a: Murmurs = abnormal heart sounds caused by: (1) Blood forced through a NARROWED (stenotic) valve opening — 'whoosh' of turbulent flow. (2) Backward flow (REGURGITATION) through a valve that did not close completely. Valvular stenosis (stenos = narrow): narrowing of a valve opening, may be inherited or result from inflammation/disease. Clinical timing: aortic stenosis murmur heard during VENTRICULAR EJECTION (Phase 4); mitral regurgitation murmur heard during VENTRICULAR SYSTOLE (Phases 3-4); aortic regurgitation murmur heard during VENTRICULAR DIASTOLE.

q: What is the WIGGERS DIAGRAM? What four tracks does it show?
a: The Wiggers diagram (Fig 14.19) summarizes all electrical and mechanical events of one cardiac cycle in aligned time tracks: (1) ECG trace (P, QRS, T waves). (2) Pressure curves — aortic pressure (80–120 mmHg with dicrotic notch), left ventricular pressure, left atrial pressure. (3) Heart sounds (S1 and S2 timing). (4) Left ventricular volume (rises to ~135 mL EDV at point E; falls to ~65 mL ESV at point F).

q: Define STROKE VOLUME (SV). Write the formula and give the normal resting value.
a: Stroke volume = amount of blood pumped by ONE ventricle during ONE contraction (mL/beat). Formula: SV = EDV - ESV. Normal resting values: 135 mL - 65 mL = 70 mL/beat.

q: Define EJECTION FRACTION. Write the formula and give the normal resting value.
a: Ejection fraction = percentage of EDV ejected with one contraction. Formula: EF = SV / EDV x 100%. Normal resting value: 70 mL / 135 mL = ~52%. During exercise with SV of 100 mL: 100/135 = ~74%.

q: Define CARDIAC OUTPUT (CO). Write the formula and calculate the normal resting value.
a: Cardiac output = volume of blood pumped by ONE ventricle in a given period of time (mL/min). Formula: CO = heart rate x stroke volume. Normal resting: 72 bpm x 70 mL/beat = ~5,040 mL/min (~5 L/min). During exercise: CO may increase to 30–35 L/min. The SAME cardiac output applies to both ventricles under normal conditions.

q: What is the significance of cardiac output vs. total blood volume? What happens if one side of the heart pumps more than the other?
a: Average total blood volume = ~5 L. At rest, one ventricle pumps all the blood in the body through it in just 1 minute (CO ≈ 5 L/min). If one side fails → CO becomes MISMATCHED → blood POOLS in the circulation behind the weaker ventricle (e.g., left ventricle failure → blood pools in pulmonary circulation → pulmonary edema).

q: Summarize the AUTONOMIC CONTROL of heart rate (overall).
a: Both divisions of the ANS innervate the SA node. Parasympathetic activity: SLOWS heart rate. Sympathetic activity: SPEEDS UP heart rate. The integrating center is the CARDIOVASCULAR CONTROL CENTER in the medulla oblongata. Tonic control is normally DOMINATED by the parasympathetic branch at rest.

q: Describe the PARASYMPATHETIC mechanism for slowing heart rate at the cellular level.
a: Parasympathetic neurons release ACh → binds MUSCARINIC cholinergic receptors on SA node cells → (1) K+ permeability INCREASES → hyperpolarizes cell → pacemaker potential starts at a MORE NEGATIVE value. (2) Ca2+ permeability DECREASES → slower rate of depolarization. Combined: cell takes LONGER to reach threshold → SLOWER heart rate. ACh also slows AV node conduction → increased AV node delay.

q: Describe the SYMPATHETIC mechanism for increasing heart rate at the cellular level.
