a: Sympathetic neurons release NE (and adrenal medulla releases epinephrine) → binds BETA-1 (β1) adrenergic receptors on SA node → activates cAMP second messenger system → increases Na+ and Ca2+ influx (speeds up If and Ca2+ channel activity) → FASTER rate of pacemaker depolarization → cell reaches threshold sooner → HIGHER heart rate. Also enhances AV node conduction.

q: What is TONIC AUTONOMIC CONTROL of heart rate? Which branch dominates at rest?
a: Tonic control = continuous background neural input to the SA node. The PARASYMPATHETIC branch normally dominates at rest. Evidence: when ALL autonomic input is blocked, the intrinsic SA node rate = ~90–100 bpm. To achieve a normal resting HR of ~70 bpm, tonic PARASYMPATHETIC activity must continuously SLOW the intrinsic rate down from ~90 bpm.

q: What are the TWO ways to increase heart rate from rest, and which is simpler?
a: (1) SIMPLER: DECREASE parasympathetic activity (withdrawal of tonic inhibition) → SA node resumes intrinsic rate → HR increases to ~90–100 bpm. (2) ADD sympathetic input: norepinephrine/epinephrine on β1-receptors → speeds up depolarization → HR can increase ABOVE the intrinsic rate (e.g., during intense exercise).

q: What are the TWO parameters that determine the force of ventricular contraction in the isolated heart?
a: (1) LENGTH of muscle fibers at the beginning of contraction (determined by EDV). (2) CONTRACTILITY of the heart (the intrinsic ability of cardiac muscle to contract at a given fiber length, determined by Ca2+ availability).

q: State the FRANK-STARLING LAW of the heart. Who first described it?
a: Frank-Starling law: within physiological limits, the HEART PUMPS ALL THE BLOOD THAT RETURNS TO IT. As additional blood enters the ventricles → muscle fibers stretch → contract MORE FORCEFULLY → eject more blood. Stroke volume is proportional to EDV. First described by Otto Frank (German physiologist); expanded by Ernest Starling (British physiologist) using isolated dog heart-lung preparations.

q: Define PRELOAD.
a: Preload = the degree of MYOCARDIAL STRETCH before contraction begins — represents the load placed on cardiac muscles BEFORE they contract. In the intact heart, preload is determined by the END-DIASTOLIC VOLUME (EDV). Greater preload → greater sarcomere stretch → greater force of contraction (Frank-Starling relationship).

q: Define venous return. How does it relate to EDV?
a: Venous return = the amount of blood returning to the heart from the venous circulation. EDV is normally determined by venous return — more venous return → higher EDV → greater preload → stronger contraction (Frank-Starling). Three factors affecting venous return: skeletal muscle pump, respiratory pump, and sympathetic innervation of veins.

q: Describe the SKELETAL MUSCLE PUMP and its effect on venous return.
a: Skeletal muscle contractions SQUEEZE VEINS (especially in the legs) → compresses them and pushes blood toward the heart. During exercise involving lower extremities: skeletal muscle pump HELPS venous return. During sitting or standing motionless: skeletal muscle pump does NOT assist (no muscle contractions to squeeze veins).

q: Describe the RESPIRATORY PUMP and its effect on venous return.
a: During INSPIRATION: chest expands and diaphragm moves toward abdomen → thoracic cavity enlarges → develops SUBATMOSPHERIC PRESSURE in thorax → decreases pressure in inferior vena cava as it passes through thorax → draws more blood from abdominal veins into thoracic veins. Also: inspiration compresses abdominal veins (higher abdominal pressure) + lower thoracic vein pressure = ENHANCED venous return during each breath.

q: How does SYMPATHETIC INNERVATION of veins affect venous return?
a: Sympathetic stimulation → venoconstriction → DECREASES vein volume → squeezes more blood into the heart → larger ventricular volume at beginning of next contraction → more forceful contraction (Frank-Starling). Allows body to redistribute venous blood to the arterial side of circulation.

q: Define CONTRACTILITY. How does it differ from the Frank-Starling (length-tension) mechanism?
a: Contractility = the intrinsic ability of a cardiac muscle fiber to contract at any given fiber length; a function of Ca2+ interaction with contractile filaments. Frank-Starling/length-tension: force changes because SARCOMERE LENGTH changes (different EDV). Contractility: force changes at the SAME sarcomere length and EDV — because more Ca2+ is available. A positive inotropic agent shifts the Starling curve UPWARD (more force at same EDV).

q: Define INOTROPIC AGENT and INOTROPIC EFFECT. Give two examples of POSITIVE inotropic agents.
a: Inotropic agent (ino = fiber): any chemical that affects the CONTRACTILITY of the heart. Inotropic effect: the influence of such a chemical on contractility. Positive inotropic effect = INCREASES force of contraction. Two examples: (1) Catecholamines — epinephrine and norepinephrine. (2) Cardiac glycosides — digitalis (digitoxin, ouabain).

q: Describe the CATECHOLAMINE MECHANISM for increasing CONTRACTILITY in cardiac contractile cells (both targets).
a: Epinephrine/norepinephrine bind to β1-adrenergic receptors → activate cAMP second messenger system → phosphorylate two targets: (1) Voltage-gated Ca2+ CHANNELS: phosphorylation → Ca2+ channels open more often and stay open longer → more Ca2+ enters from ECF → more CICR from SR → more Ca2+ on troponin → MORE FORCEFUL contraction. (2) PHOSPHOLAMBAN: phosphorylation → enhances Ca2+-ATPase on SR → concentrates more Ca2+ in SR → more available for CICR; also SPEEDS Ca2+ removal from cytosol → SHORTENS duration of contraction.

q: What is PHOSPHOLAMBAN? What is its role in cardiac contractility?
a: Phospholamban = a regulatory protein associated with the sarcoplasmic reticulum Ca2+-ATPase. In its UNPHOSPHORYLATED state: inhibits the SR Ca2+-ATPase, slowing Ca2+ uptake into SR. When PHOSPHORYLATED (by catecholamine-activated cAMP): inhibition is relieved → Ca2+-ATPase activity INCREASES → more Ca2+ stored in SR → more Ca2+ available for CICR → stronger contraction; also speeds Ca2+ removal → shorter contraction duration.

q: What are CARDIAC GLYCOSIDES? What is their mechanism and clinical use?
a: Cardiac glycosides (e.g., digitalis/digitoxin from Digitalis purpurea [purple foxglove]): POSITIVE inotropic drugs used since the 18th century for HEART FAILURE. Mechanism: inhibit Na+-K+-ATPase → Na+ builds up in cell → reduces the Na+ gradient → decreases ability to remove Ca2+ via NCX → cytosolic Ca2+ increases → MORE FORCEFUL contractions. Contrast with catecholamines: catecholamines SPEED Ca2+ removal; glycosides SLOW it. Note: highly toxic drugs.

q: Define AFTERLOAD. What clinical conditions increase afterload?
a: Afterload = the combined load of blood in the ventricle (EDV) AND the ARTERIAL RESISTANCE the ventricle must overcome during contraction. The ventricle must create enough pressure to DISPLACE blood already filling the aorta and push it downstream. Increased afterload is found with: (1) elevated arterial blood pressure and (2) loss of compliance (stretchability) in the aorta. To maintain SV despite increased afterload → heart must INCREASE force of contraction → greater O2 and ATP consumption.

q: What is a MYOCARDIAL INFARCTION (MI)? What causes it?
a: Myocardial infarction (MI) = an area of heart muscle damaged or dying because of LACK OF BLOOD SUPPLY (ischemia). Caused by partial or complete blockage (often atherosclerosis) of a coronary artery, reducing blood flow to a region of myocardium. Without adequate O2, that region cannot contract and may die (necrosis).

q: What is S-T SEGMENT DEPRESSION? What is Non-STEMI?
a: S-T segment depression = the ST segment drops BELOW the ECG baseline — often caused by decreased blood flow (ischemia) to a small area of the heart. Non-STEMI (Non-ST Elevation MI) = damage to a small part of the heart from SEVERE NARROWING (but not complete blockage) of a heart artery. Diagnosed by ST depression + elevated blood TROPONIN levels.

q: Why do elevated blood levels of TROPONIN indicate heart damage?
a: Troponin = a regulatory protein of striated muscle bound to tropomyosin — normally found INSIDE cardiac cells. Elevated blood troponin indicates that cardiac cells have been DAMAGED or are DEAD, releasing their intracellular proteins into the bloodstream. Cardiac troponin (cTnI or cTnT) is highly specific for cardiac muscle damage.

q: What is the mechanism of NITROGLYCERIN in the coronary circulation?
a: Nitroglycerin is metabolized to NITRIC OXIDE (NO), which causes VASODILATION of blood vessels — relaxes smooth muscle in coronary artery walls → INCREASES coronary blood flow → reduces ischemia and chest pain (angina). Used acutely for angina and MI.

q: What is a BETA BLOCKER's effect on the heart? Why is it used after a heart attack?
a: Beta blockers are ANTAGONISTS to β1-adrenergic receptors → DECREASE heart rate (block sympathetic acceleration) AND DECREASE contractility (block positive inotropic effect) → REDUCE cardiac output → LOWER OXYGEN DEMAND. Used after MI because cells with lower O2 demand are LESS LIKELY TO DIE if their blood supply is reduced.

q: How do CALCIUM CHANNEL BLOCKERS reduce cardiac output?
a: Calcium channel blockers reduce Ca2+ ENTRY into myocardial cells → reduce contractility (negative inotropic effect) → reduce stroke volume → reduce CO. Can also slow pacemaker depolarization rate (reduce heart rate) by reducing Ca2+ influx in SA node cells.

q: What is IVABRADINE? What is its mechanism and clinical use?
a: Ivabradine = a drug that SELECTIVELY BLOCKS If channels in SA node pacemaker cells. Blocking If channels → slows the rate of pacemaker depolarization → DECREASES HEART RATE. Used for conditions requiring heart rate reduction such as certain types of angina or heart failure. Does NOT affect contractility (unlike beta blockers).

q: CALCULATION: Given HR = 80 bpm and SV = 65 mL/beat, calculate CO.
a: CO = HR x SV = 80 bpm x 65 mL/beat = 5,200 mL/min (5.2 L/min).

q: CALCULATION: Given EDV = 150 mL and SV = 65 mL, calculate ESV.
a: ESV = EDV - SV = 150 mL - 65 mL = 85 mL.

q: CALCULATION: Given ejection fraction = 25% and SV = 40 mL, calculate EDV and ESV. Also calculate CO if HR = 100 bpm.
a: SV = EDV x EF → 40 mL = EDV x 0.25 → EDV = 160 mL. ESV = EDV - SV = 160 - 40 = 120 mL. CO = SV x HR = 40 mL x 100 bpm = 4,000 mL/min (4 L/min).

q: Table 14.2: What does the RIGHT ATRIUM receive blood from and send it to?
a: Receives from: venae cavae (superior and inferior). Sends to: right ventricle.

q: Table 14.2: What does the LEFT VENTRICLE receive blood from and send it to?
a: Receives from: left atrium. Sends to: the body (systemic circulation) via the AORTA — everything except the lungs.

q: Table 14.2: What does the PULMONARY TRUNK receive blood from and send it to?
a: Receives from: right ventricle. Sends to: lungs (left and right via left and right pulmonary arteries).

q: Why does blood circulate in a one-way circuit? What prevents reversal?
a: A system of VALVES in the heart and veins ensures blood flows in only ONE DIRECTION — acting like turnstiles. In the heart: AV valves and semilunar valves. In veins: venous valves. This one-way flow ensures systematic distribution of gases, nutrients, signal molecules, and wastes.

q: During ventricular CONTRACTION, which valves are open and which are closed? During ventricular RELAXATION?
a: During ventricular CONTRACTION (ejection phase): AV valves CLOSED (prevent backflow to atria); semilunar valves OPEN (blood exits to arteries). During ventricular RELAXATION (isovolumic relaxation): semilunar valves CLOSED (prevent backflow from arteries); AV valves also CLOSED initially, then OPEN when ventricular pressure falls below atrial pressure.

q: Why can autorhythmic cells initiate action potentials WITHOUT innervation from the nervous system?
a: Autorhythmic cells have an UNSTABLE membrane potential (pacemaker potential) that spontaneously drifts toward threshold. This is driven by If channels (open at negative potentials, allowing net Na+ influx) and Ca2+ channels. No external signal is needed — the cell's own ion channel properties cause it to depolarize to threshold repeatedly and automatically.

q: Explain how the AUTONOMIC NERVOUS SYSTEM modulates heart rate by altering the pacemaker potential (for BOTH divisions with cellular detail).
a: Parasympathetic (ACh → muscarinic receptors): increases K+ efflux (hyperpolarizes cell) and decreases Ca2+ influx (slows depolarization) → cell starts more negative AND depolarizes more slowly → takes longer to reach threshold → SLOWER rate. Sympathetic (NE/Epi → β1 receptors → cAMP): increases Na+ and Ca2+ influx through If and Ca2+ channels → cell depolarizes MORE RAPIDLY → reaches threshold sooner → FASTER rate.

q: Describe the 4 segments of the LEFT VENTRICULAR PRESSURE-VOLUME CURVE (A→B→C→D→A).
a: A → B: Ventricular filling (passive + atrial contraction) — volume INCREASES, pressure barely rises. B = EDV; AV valve closes. B → C: Isovolumic contraction — pressure INCREASES rapidly, volume UNCHANGED. C: Aortic valve opens. C → D: Ventricular ejection — pressure continues up then drops; volume DECREASES. D = ESV; aortic valve closes. D → A: Isovolumic relaxation — pressure DECREASES, volume UNCHANGED. Back to A where AV valve opens.

q: What is a 12-LEAD ECG? Why are multiple leads needed?
a: 12-lead ECG = standard clinical ECG using various combinations of three limb electrodes PLUS six electrodes placed on the chest and trunk. Multiple leads are needed because the heart is a THREE-DIMENSIONAL electrical generator — different leads provide different 'views' giving information about different regions of the heart. Useful for detecting MI location, conduction abnormalities, hypertrophy, and arrhythmias.

q: What are the FOUR key clinical questions to ask when interpreting any ECG?
a: (1) What is the HEART RATE? (normal = 60–100 bpm). (2) Is the RHYTHM regular or irregular? (3) Are all normal waves (P, QRS, T) present in recognizable form? (4) Is there ONE QRS complex for EACH P wave? — if not, or if P-R segment varies, suggests AV node conduction problem.

q: Describe the ECG appearance of: (a) atrial fibrillation and (b) ventricular fibrillation.
a: (a) Atrial fibrillation: NO discernible P waves; irregular, rapid undulations of baseline; IRREGULAR R-R intervals. (b) Ventricular fibrillation: completely CHAOTIC, irregular waveforms — no recognizable P, QRS, or T waves. Immediately life-threatening.

q: Describe THIRD-DEGREE (complete) HEART BLOCK on ECG. What does it indicate?
a: Third-degree heart block ECG: P waves and QRS complexes are DISSOCIATED — P waves occur at the normal (faster) SA node rate while QRS complexes occur at a much SLOWER ventricular rate. Many P waves for each QRS. Indicates complete failure of AV node conduction — atria and ventricles are beating independently.

q: What is the CORONARY SINUS? Where is it located and where does it drain?
a: The coronary sinus = a venous reservoir on the POSTERIOR surface of the heart that collects approximately 85% of coronary venous blood. It drains directly into the RIGHT ATRIUM.

q: When can a normal jugular venous pulse be observed? What does an abnormal jugular pulse suggest?
a: Normal: a small retrograde (backward) pulse in the jugular vein is observable in a person lying with head and chest elevated ~30 degrees (blood forced backward into veins during atrial systole). ABNORMAL: an observable jugular pulse in a person sitting UPRIGHT suggests that pressure in the RIGHT ATRIUM is HIGHER than normal — potential sign of right heart failure or elevated venous pressure.

q: What is the INTRINSIC rate of the SA node when ALL autonomic input is blocked?
a: ~90–100 beats per minute. Because the resting heart rate is ~70 bpm (below the intrinsic rate), it means tonic PARASYMPATHETIC activity is CONTINUOUSLY slowing the heart down from its intrinsic rate. This demonstrates that parasympathetic tone DOMINATES at rest.

q: What is the average RESTING HEART RATE? What is normal range? How do trained athletes and children differ?
a: Average resting HR: ~70 bpm. Normal range: 60–100 bpm (athletes often 60 bpm with ECG). Trained athletes: may have resting HR of 50 bpm or LESS (strong parasympathetic tone; each beat has higher SV due to cardiac hypertrophy). Children: have HIGHER average heart rates than adults.

q: Describe Starling's experimental finding with the isolated heart-lung preparation. What happened at VERY HIGH venous pressures?
a: Starling's finding: in the absence of nervous or hormonal control, the heart pumped ALL the blood returned to it — stroke volume increased as venous pressure (and EDV) increased. At VERY HIGH venous pressures: cardiac output DECREASED — because excessive stretch causes sarcomeres to work on the DESCENDING LIMB of the length-tension curve (over-stretching reduces myosin-actin overlap → less force).

q: Why do blood enter AND exit the ventricles at the TOP? What embryonic event causes this?
a: During development, the TUBULAR EMBRYONIC HEART TWISTS BACK ON ITSELF, bringing the arterial exits (originally at the bottom of the tube) close to the top of the ventricles. Functionally, this means ventricles must CONTRACT FROM BOTTOM UP (apex to base) to squeeze blood out the openings at the top.

q: Name the TWO key functions of the AV node.
a: (1) Acts as the ONLY electrical pathway through which action potentials can reach the contractile fibers of the ventricles (the fibrous skeleton blocks all other paths). (2) DELAYS transmission of APs — allows atria to COMPLETE contraction before ventricular contraction begins (ensures complete ventricular filling).

q: Why can an ECG be recorded from the body SURFACE?
a: Because the body's extracellular fluid (NaCl-based salt solution) is a good CONDUCTOR of electricity. Electrical signals generated in the heart propagate through this conducting fluid to the body surface, where electrodes can detect them.

q: CLINICAL: What is AUSCULTATION and what does it assess?
a: Auscultation (auscultare = to listen to) = listening to the heart through the chest wall using a STETHOSCOPE (placed against chest and back). Normally reveals two audible sounds: S1 (lub — AV valve closure) and S2 (dup — semilunar valve closure). Abnormal sounds include gallops (S3 or S4), clicks (abnormal valve movement), and murmurs (stenosis or incompetent valves).

q: The memory aid 'R-S-T' helps remember which AV valve. What does it stand for, and which valve is on the OTHER side?
a: R-S-T = Right Side has the Tricuspid (3 flaps). The OTHER side (left side) has the BICUSPID (mitral) valve (2 flaps). Alternative: Remember 'MItral = MItral' is on the LEFT, or it has only 2 flaps like a bishop's MIter.

q: Summarize ALL factors that INCREASE stroke volume.
a: SV increases with: (1) Increased EDV (more venous return → Frank-Starling; via skeletal muscle pump, respiratory pump, sympathetic venoconstriction). (2) Increased contractility (via catecholamines binding β1 → cAMP → more Ca2+ via channels + phospholamban; or digitalis slowing Ca2+ removal). (3) Decreased afterload (less arterial resistance). SV decreases with damage to ventricular myocardium, decreased venous return, or increased afterload.
